coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@Fe2O3/multiwall carbon nanotubes ternary nanocomposite for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of 5-fluorouracil, uric acid, and xanthine
coredata.dc:creator: Ganesan, Muthusankar
Ramadhass, Keerthika Devi
Chuang, Ho-Chiao
Gopalakrishnan, Gopu
coredata.dcterms:subject: 5-fluorouracil
Uric acid
Xanthine
Electrochemical sensor
Anticancer drug

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite and electrode modification
Structural characterization of nanocomposite & ternary nanocomposite
Electrocatalytic activity of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE towards 5-FU, XA, and UA
Effect of scan rate and pH

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: HR-TEM images of N-CQDs at different magnifications (a) 5 nm (inset: SAED pattern) (b) expanded view of (a) (insert: lattice fringes) (c) XPS wide spectrum of N-CQDs and narrow scans of (d) C 1 s (e) N 1 s and (f) O 1 s.

(a) PXRD patterns of N-CQD and N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite. HR-TEM images of N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite in the magnifications of (b) 50 nm (c) 20 nm. (d) Elemental mapping for all elements present in the nanocomposite. Electron mapping for (e) Fe, (f) O, (g) C, and (h) N. HR-TEM images of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT in a different magnification (i)100 nm (j) 20 nm (k) 10 nm and its (l) SAED pattern.

In 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- containing 0.1 M KCl solution (a) EIS curves of different modified electrodes at the frequency range from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz (b) CV responses of different modified electrodes at 50 mV s−1 (c) CV responses of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE at the different scan from 10 to 120 mV s−1 and (d) the corresponding linear plot of redox current vs. square root of the scan rate.

(a) CV responses of UA, XA, and 5-FU (100 μM each) at the bare and modified electrodes in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0), scan rate 50 mV s−1, in a similar condition (b) the current response with the different loading volume of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT, and (c) at a varying concentration of analyte from 50 to 200 μM.
CVs of UA, XA, and 5-FU (100 μM each) on N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE (a) with the varying scan rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1 in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) (b) at different pH conditions.

DPV responses obtained at N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE in PBS (pH 7.0) for the successive addition of (a) UA (c) XA (e) 5-FU by fixing other analytes at constant, (b, d, f) The calibration plot for the cathodic peak current vs. concentration of UA, XA, and 5-FU respectively.
(a) DPVs for changing the concentration of UA, XA, and 5-FU at N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT in 0.1 M PBS (pH 7.0) and (b) corresponding calibration plots.
(a) DPV responses of UA, XA, and 5-FU in the presence of potentially interfering species and (b-d) their corresponding bar diagram for UA, XA, and 5-FU, respectively.

Bar diagram for the DPV signals of (a) reproducibility study at five modified electrodes (b) repeatability tests of the N CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE at one electrode (c) storage stability tests of the sensor upto one week.
DPV signals were obtained at the surface of N CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE for UA and XA in the raw urine sample.
Schematic representation for the synthesis of ternary nanocomposite material, electrode fabrication process, and the simultaneous electrochemical detection of 5-FU, UA, and XA.
Schematic representation for the electro-oxidation mechanism of UA, XA, and 5-FU.
Comparison of analytical parameters for the determination of 5-FU.
Comparison of analytical parameters for the determination of UA & XA.
The recovery percentage of UA, XA, and 5-FU in urine sample using N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots@Fe2O3/multiwall carbon nanotubes ternary nanocomposite for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of 5-fluorouracil, uric acid, and xanthine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nano & Computational Materials Lab, Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi 630003, Tamil Nadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nano & Computational Materials Lab, Department of Industrial Chemistry, Alagappa University, Karaikudi-630003, Tamil Nadu, India.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan.

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, College of Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, No. 1, Section 3, Chung-Hsiao East Road, Taipei, Taiwan 106 (ROC)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, National Taipei University of Technology, Taipei, Taiwan.

ce:section.ce:para: Uric acid (UA), xanthine (XA) are co-existing biomolecules produced as degradation products during human purine metabolism. 5-fluorouracil (5-fluoro-1H-pyrimidine-2,4-dione, 5-FU) is one of the most important UA derivatives reported as a potential cancer drug for cancer treatment worldwide. The three molecules UA, XA, and 5-FU, are similar in structure [1–3]. UA is a well-known biomolecule that acts as a scavenger for hydroxyl radicals and singlet oxygen in the biological system. However, its excess concentration in the bloodstream causes urate crystals' deposition, leading to renal impairment and the joints' chronic inflammation [4]. Besides, XA is the key metabolite of adenine nucleotide degradation and a biomarker for diseases such as adult respiratory distress syndrome, gout, renal failure, and tumor hyperthermia [5]. During purine degradation, XA can be transformed into UA, where XA is an intermediate and UA is the final product. Ribonucleic acid (RNA) consists of a pyrimidine base where uracil forms a base pair with adenine. Nucleic acid biosynthesis is primarily associated with uracil in tumor cells [6]. When halogens replace the uracil in fifth place, it has an enormous biological effect on tumor cells [7]. Among the derivatives, 5-FU is an effective drug for the stomach, breast, and cervical cancer worldwide [8]. The 5-FU metabolites prevent the thymidylate synthase's function and ultimately prevent the DNA replication [9]. The prescribed dose level of 5-FU in the human body ranges from 13.007 to 130.007 ng/mL [10]. But if the concentration exceeds, it causes severe side effects such as neurotoxicity, cardiotoxicity, and mortality [11]. The quantification of UA, XA, and 5-FU concentrations in the biological fluid is therefore essential for diagnosing and medical management of many diseases.

Several analytical techniques, including chromatography [12], spectrophotometry [13], and electrochemical [14] methods, have been used for the simultaneous determination of UA and XA and the individual determination of 5-FU. However, the simultaneous electrochemical determination of UA, XA, and 5-FU has not yet been reported. This is due to structural similarity and cross-interference issues in the analysis. Among the various analytical methods, electrochemical determination has been identified as an excellent technique for sensitive and simultaneous quantification of analytes due to its simplicity, quick response, and low cost. Analytes UA, XA, and 5-FU are structurally similar, and their oxidation potentials are close to each other, making it impossible to measure simultaneously with bare glass carbon electrodes (GCE). In addition to that, oxidized products may be adsorbed on the electrode surface and decrease the electrode's sensitivity and selectivity [15]. The advanced electrode material is, therefore, needed for simultaneous determination.

To overcome these issues, nanostructured materials, such as transitional metal oxides, have been widely used for electrochemical sensing applications due to an excellent electrocatalytic nature, which can be further amplified by chemical doping [16] and composite formation [17]. In terms of transitional metal oxides, iron oxide nanoparticles (Fe2O3 NPs) have been gaining wide research attention due to their tunable banding, electrocatalytic properties, biocompatibility, and natural abundance [18]. In literature, it is reported that the direct electron transfer rate can be increased when Fe2O3 NPs are incorporated into carbon nanomaterials such as graphene [19], graphene oxide [20], and nanotubes [21]. Our research group has recently reported that the rate of charge transfer at electrode/electrolyte interface can be improved when metal oxides are hybridized with nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) [22]. Because the N-CQDs separate the produced charges at the interface of the electrode surface and offer a quick transfer of charge through the modified electrodes. Also, the N-CQDs possess a graphite conjugate structure, through which it can make the π-π interaction with other carbonaceous nanomaterials to form the ternary nanocomposites. Therefore, it is proposed that the N-CQDs embedded in Fe2O3 NPs and their hybridization with MWCNTs result in functional nanohybrid material and are expected to be an excellent electrocatalyst for the simultaneous determination of UA, XA, and 5-FU. The existence of MWCNTs can provide high mechanical strength and stability to the ternary nanocomposite. From these perspectives, we construct the ternary nanocomposite by combining N-CQDs, Fe2O3 NPs, and MWCNTs for the first time. Precisely, the N-CQDs loaded Fe2O3 nanocomposite was synthesized using a hydrothermal method. Then it was hybridized with MWCNTs using an ultrasonication method to resulted in the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite. This functional nanohybrid material was utilized as an electrode modifier, and its simultaneous sensing performance is reported here.

All the analytical grade reagents procured from SRL chemicals, India. Ethylenediamine (EDA, C2H8N2), citric acid (CA, C6H8O7), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3.6H2O), uric acid (UA, C5H4N4O3), xanthine (XA, C5H4N4O2), 5-fluorouracil (5-FU, C4H3FN2O2), sodium acetate (C2H3NaO2), polyethylene glycol (PEG 4000). All the chemicals were used in the received condition. The stock solution of UA, XA, and 5-FU were prepared in hot-water (60 °C). Phosphate buffer was prepared using disodium hydrogen phosphate dihydrate (Na2HPO4.2H2O) and sodium dihydrogen phosphate dihydrate (NaH2PO4.2H2O) and used as a supporting electrolyte. The pH of the buffer solution was adjusted with NaOH or HCl as per the need. The deionized water (18 MΩ) was utilized for the preparation of solutions.

The N-CQD synthesis process was adopted from our previous report [23]. In short, 2 g of citric acid and 5 mL of EDA were dissolved and well stirred in 50 mL of distilled water. The homogenous solution was then subjected to microwave irradiation using a domestic microwave oven (700 W) for 10 min. The resultant reddish-brown gel was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water. The obtained reddish-brown solution was ultra-centrifuged for 15 min at 10000 rpm to remove unreacted particles. The supernatant solution was then exposed to membrane filtration using a 0.22 μm pore-sized syringe filter resulting in a fine N-CQD solution.

Approximately 0.4 g of FeCl3.6H2O was dissolved in 16 mL of ethylene glycol and well stirred. To this solution, the surfactants such as sodium acetate (1 g) and PEG 4000 (0.4 g) were added and vigorously stirred to produce a homogeneous solution. These surfactants act as shape-inducing capping molecules during the synthesis process [24,25]. Then 7.5 mL of N-CQD was added and stirred for 1 h. This mixture solution was then transferred to 100 mL of stainless-steel autoclave and heat-treated at 200 °C for 8 h in a hot-air furnace. After being treated, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally. The red residue obtained was exposed to triple wash with water and ethanol. The washed product was dried at 80 °C to obtain a pure nanocomposite of N-CQD/Fe2O3. For comparison, Fe2O3 NPs were synthesized using a similar procedure, with the exception of N-CQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite and electrode modification
ce:section.ce:para: About 5 mg of N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite and 5 mg of MWCNT were dispersed in 1 mL of isopropanol and treated with ultrasonic waves using a bath sonicator for 1 h. The obtained black-colored suspension was known as N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite. The optimized volume of 5 μL was pipetted out from a black-colored suspension and coated on GCE's clean surface (3 mm Dia.) using a drop cast method. The GCE was then allowed to dry at room temperature to obtain a modified electrode of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE (N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE). The synthesis and fabrication procedures and their simultaneous detection of analytes are schematically displayed in Scheme 1 .

The diffraction pattern and functional groups of the samples were documented using X-ray diffraction with Cu Kα radiation (λ = 1.540 Å) (Bruker AXS D8 diffractometer) and FT-IR spectrometers (JASCO FT/IR - 4600), respectively. The surface morphology and crystallinity were analyzed using the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM; Jeol/JEM 2100) and selective area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns, respectively. Electrochemical studies have been conducted using the CH instrument. An electrochemical cell consists of a conventional three-electrode system. A bare GCE or modified GCE acts as a working electrode, a platinum wire as a counter electrode, and a saturated Ag/AgCl electrode as a reference electrode. 0.1 M concentration of phosphate buffer solution (PBS) was used as an electrolyte, and its pH value was adjusted using NaOH or HCl as required.

The electrochemical behavior of the modified electrodes was characterized using cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) techniques in a 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of [Fe(CN)6]3−/4- redox probe. The CV measurements were performed between the potential window from −0.4 to 0.8 V at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1. At the same time, the EIS technique was performed in the frequency range from 100 mHz to 100 kHz at the applied potential of 5 mV. The modified electrodes' electrocatalytic behavior towards analytes was studied using CV and differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) techniques. The CVs and DPVs were recorded in an optimized potential window of 0 to 1.1 V using PBS as a buffer solution (pH 7.0) at the scan rate of 50 mV s−1.

The HR-TEM images of N-CQDs indicate spherical morphology with uniform distribution, as shown in Fig. 1a, b. From the histogram plot (Fig. S1), the size of N-CQDs was measured, and it was ranged from 5 to 10 nm. The N-CQDs show the lattice fringes (Fig. 1b) in their structure with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which is close to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite carbon [6], confirming the graphite-like structure of N-CQDs. Furthermore, N-CQDs' SAED pattern (inset: Fig. 1a) shows the rings and a few bright spots, which correspond to amorphous and crystalline structures, respectively. The PXRD pattern of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2a. A broad diffraction peak centered at 27° (d002 = 0.35 nm) and weak diffraction positioned at 46° (d100 = 0.22 nm) confirm the amorphous carbon phase and the existence of fractional graphite structure in the carbon core of N-CQDs, which suggest the presence of rich functional groups in its structure [26]. All the above results reveal that the N-CQDs have been successfully obtained from the microwave-assisted synthesis method, and they have a graphitic structure.

Furthermore, the N-doping and chemical composition of N-CQDs were examined by XPS analysis. The XPS survey spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 1c) shows the three peaks at the binding energies (BEs) of 285.6, 400.2, and 533.5 eV, which are correspond to the elements such as C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The observed strong N 1 s peak also evident that the formation of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots. A deconvoluted spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 1d) exhibits four distinct peaks of different C species. The intense peak at the BE of 284.59 eV emerges from sp2 (C=C) and sp3 (C–C) hybridized carbons, while the other peaks at the BEs of 285.72, 286.95, and 287.86 eV are attributed to C–N, C–O, and C=O or C=N bonds, respectively.

In the spectrum of N 1 s (Fig. 1e), a significant peak at 399.5 eV confirms the amino N in the form of N-(C)3. It is also evident that nitrogen has been successfully incorporated into the structure of N-CQDs. The deconvoluted spectrum of O1s shows four peaks (Fig. 1f) at the BEs of 530.90, 532.12, 535.26, and 537.64 eV, which can be assigned to the carbon single bond oxygen (C–O), carbon double bond oxygen (C=O), carbon-hydroxyl group (C–OH), and carbon‑oxygen‑carbon (C–O–C) bonds, observed respectively. Thus, the XPS results confirm the successful formation of N-CQDs, with nitrogen doping and rich oxygen functional groups.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structural characterization of nanocomposite & ternary nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The PXRD pattern of N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite (Fig. 2a) showed strong diffraction peaks of the atomic planes (012), (104), (110), (113), (202), (024), (116), (122), (214), (300), and (1010), which are agreed with the rhombohedral crystal phase of α-Fe2O3 and well harmonized with standard data (JCPDS #33–0664) [27]. Meanwhile, the interaction of N-CQDs into the crystal lattice of Fe2O3 was confirmed by the existence of a broad diffraction peak centered at 26°, which is corresponding to the (002) plan of graphitic structure. As a result, it is evident that the effective integration of N-CQDs on Fe2O3. The structure of as-synthesized N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite is shown in typical HR-TEM images (Fig. 2b–d). The HR-TEM images evidently show the nano spherical-like morphology of N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite with uniform shape and size.

Further, the nanocomposite's average size was measured to be in the range of 40–60 nm (Fig. S2). In addition to that, the black dots were found to be scattered on the surface, visually evident that the presence of N-CQDs in the nanocomposite. Elemental mapping of the N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite is shown in Fig. 2d–h, which clearly specifies that the major constituents such as Fe, O, C, and N are present and evenly distributed in the nanocomposite.

The HR-TEM images of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT nanocomposite at various magnifications are shown in Fig. 2i–k. It clearly shows the excellent integration of N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite onto the sidewalls of MWCNTs. N-CQD's presence in the nanocomposite makes a π- π interaction with MWCNTs through its graphitic π conjugated structure and leads to form the stable N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite [17,23,28]. The existence of N-CQDs in the ternary nanocomposite is marked in the yellow-colored dotted lines (Fig. 2k). Furthermore, the SAED pattern of the ternary nanocomposite (Fig. 2l) exhibits more bright spots in each fringe, which reveals the polycrystalline nature. Thus, all the above results indicate that the methods followed in this work effectively synthesize both N-CQD@Fe2O3 nanocomposite and N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite.

The electrochemical active surface area and charge transfer kinetics at the interface between the working electrode and electrolyte were examined using EIS and CV techniques in a working electrolyte of 0.1 M KCl solution containing 5 mM of ferricyanide system ([Fe(CN)6]3−/4-). The EIS study gives an exact quantitative measurement of electron transfer resistance (R
ct ) at the electrode surface. Thus, the EIS measurements were performed to the electrodes such as bare GCE, Fe2O3/GCE, N-CQD@Fe2O3/GCE, MWCNT/GCE, and N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE, and their corresponding values are presented in Table S1. The observed EIS profiles in the form of the Nyquist plot with corresponding its Randles circuit are shown in Fig. 3a. The radius of the semicircles is reflecting the direct measure of the R
ct . Compared to the bare GCE, Fe2O3/GCE shows a higher semicircle, which indicates the high R
ct of the electrode. Whereas the N-CQD@Fe2O3/GCE exhibits low R
ct than bare and Fe2O3/GCE, it can be attributed to N-CQDs' presence in the Fe2O3 matrix, which improves the electron transport on its surface. Further, MWCNT/GCE and N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE are nearly show the straight line, suggesting very low R
ct of the electrodes. Moreover, it is evident that the ternary nanocomposite modified GCE exhibits a lower R
ct value than that of all compared electrodes. The results obviously prove the main objective of this work: incorporating N-CQDs in the matrix of Fe2O3 and its hybridization with MWCNT extremely decreasing the R
ct of Fe2O3 might be interrelated to the excellent conducting nature of both N-CQDs and MWCNTs.

Fig. 3b displays the CVs of modified electrodes in the standard solution. The peak-to-peak separation (ΔE
p = E
pa -
E
pc ) values 134, 127, 106, 83, and 79 mV are respectively exhibited by bare GCE, Fe2O3/GCE, N-CQD@Fe2O3/GCE, MWCNT/GCE, and N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE. The observed low ΔE
p value of the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE with higher peak current response indicates excellent reversibility, fast electron transport, and higher active surface area (Aeff). The different scan rate study in standard solution was performed (Fig. 3c), from which the Aeff of the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE was calculated using Randles-Sevick equation (Eq. 1) [29].
(1)
I p
=
2.687 ×
10 5
n
3 / 2
v
1 / 2
D
1 / 2
AC
where I
pa , n, v, D, A, and C° are referring to anodic peak current, a number of electron transfer, scan rate, diffusion coefficient, active surface area, and electrolyte concentration, respectively. Consequently, the found Aeff of bare GCE, Fe2O3/GCE, N-CQD@Fe2O3/GCE, MWCNT/GCE, and N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE are 0.039, 0.076, 0.098, 0.138, and 0.160 cm2, respectively. Further, the linear plot (Fig. 3d) between the redox current and square root of the scan rate indicates that the diffusion-controlled process occurs at the modified electrode surface in the standard solution. Finally, the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE was found as an outstanding electrocatalyst with a quick electron transfer with a higher surface area; thus, it is more advantageous for the detection of electroactive species.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrocatalytic activity of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE towards 5-FU, XA, and UA
ce:section.ce:para: 5-FU is structurally related to co-existing bio-molecules such as XA and UA. At most of the solid electrodes, the oxidation potential of these species is close to each other. Hence, the detailed investigation of the electrochemical performance of 5-FU, XA, and UA is essential. Besides, the sensitive electrode material for these molecules' simultaneous determination is also a significant task in sensor fabrication development. The CVs of bare and modified GCEs in PBS (pH 7.0) containing 100 μM of 5-FU, XA, and UA at a scan rate of 50 mV s−1 are shown in Fig. 4a. Bare GCE exhibited very weak overlapped peaks for the analytes. The Fe2O3/GCE gave three small peaks with low current response. However, N-CQD@Fe2O3/GCE showed three distinct peaks with enhanced peak current, observed at 0.25, 0.60, and 1.00 V for UA, XA, and 5-FU, respectively. The better electron transfer proficiency of N-CQD@Fe2O3/GCE than Fe2O3/GCE is due to N-CQDs' presence, which distinguishes the produced charges at the interface of the electrode surface. Thus, it offers a fast charge transfer through the modified electrodes [30]. Similarly, MWCNT/GCE provides higher electron transport with well-distinguished oxidation peaks for analytes, owing to its higher surface area. On the other hand, at the surface of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE, improved current responses with low oxidation potentials were observed at 0.23, 0.58, and 0.96 V for UA, XA, and 5-FU, respectively. Moreover, well separated anodic peak with the peak-to-peak separation potentials of 0.35 V (UA - XA), 0.38 V (XA - 5-FU), and 0.73 V (UA – 5-FU) were also observed. As a result, each analyte can be identified without any cross-interference. This excellent electrocatalytic performance might be due to the synchronous interaction of Fe2O3, N-CQDs, and MWCNTs in all in one form. Furthermore, the electrochemical response for the different quantities of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT (1, 3, 5, 7, and 9 μL) on GCE's surface was measured. The obtained results (Fig. 4b) suggesting that different quantities of the active material are favorably influencing the simultaneous sensing of UA, XA, and 5-FU.

From the optimization experiment, it was found that the 5 μL of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite exhibits a higher current response than other loading volumes such as 1, 3, 7, and 9 μL. Beyond the increase of 5 μL loading amount leads to a decrease in the analytes' oxidation peak current. A higher loading concentration of catalyst may hinder the electron transfer process to the active sites and interface between the electrode and analyte [31,32]. Thus, 5 μL loading was chosen as an optimized amount of active material for simultaneous sensitive sensing. The CVs at the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE surface during the increasing concentration of UA, XA, and 5-FU from 50 to 250 μM are displayed in Fig. 4c. As seen in Fig. 4c, the anodic peak currents of analytes are increased with increasing concentration without any deformations, suggesting the excellent anti-fouling nature of the ternary nanocomposite modified GCE. All the above CV results reveal that the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE possesses an efficient electrocatalytic performance towards the oxidation of UA, XA, and 5-FU, which can be credited to the unique structural arrangements and outstanding electrochemical behavior of the ternary nanocomposite. The presence of N-CQDs in N-CQD@Fe2O3 makes well interaction with MWCNTs in the ternary nanocomposite. Thus, it synergistically contributed to the tremendous improvement in the simultaneous electrochemical sensing of three structurally similar analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of scan rate and pH
ce:section.ce:para: The electrochemical determination of analytes is influenced by the different scan rates and the working electrolyte's pH. Thus, the optimization studies on different scan rates and pH were performed at N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE. The CVs (Fig. 5a) of 100 μM UA, XA, and 5-FU in PBS (pH 7.0) with changing the scan rate from 10 to 100 mV s−1, where anodic peak current of three analytes increases with increasing scan rate. As a consequence, the insert figure of Fig. 5a represents the linear relationship between the scan rate and anodic peak current of three analytes, which suggests that the over-all sensing process of UA, XA, and 5-FU on N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE surface by adsorption-controlled process. Moreover, the linear regression equation of obtained linear plots can be expressed as follows;

The change in anodic peak potential (Epa) by varying the scan rate (v) corresponds to the electrode process's irreversibility. As the strong adsorption of analytes and irreversibility of the electrode process, Laviron eq. (2) was used to calculate the number of electrons involved in the reaction.
(2)
Ep = E ° +
2.303 RT
1 − α
nF
logv
where α is the transferring coefficient, n is the number of electrons transferred, E° is the formal potential, and v is the scan rate. The remaining parameters are constants (R = 8.314 JK−1 mol−1, T = 298 K, F = 96,480C mol−1). The value of E° can be gained from the intercept of the Ep vs. v plot (Fig. S3). The αn value can be calculated using the slope of Ep vs. log v plot (Fig. S4), and it was found to be 1.3, 1.2, and 1.2 for UA, XA, and 5-FU, respectively. Generally, the value of α can be assumed to be 0.5 for the irreversible electrode process; hence the n was calculated to be two. Thus, it indicates that the two-electrons (2e−) have contributed to the electrocatalytic oxidation of UA, XA, and 5-FU. The possible electrochemical reaction mechanisms are shown in Scheme 2 , which are well aligned with the literature reports [2,6,12,14].

The CVs of UA, XA, and 5-FU (150 μM each) in various pH solutions range from 3 to 11 at the surface of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE are shown in Fig. 5b. Interestingly, 5-FU oxidizes only at pH 7.0; there is no responses were observed at other pH solutions. In comparison, UA and XA's anodic peak potentials were negatively shifted when the solution's pH changed from 3 to 11. Notably, all three analytes were giving the higher peak current responses at pH 7.0, suggesting that at neutral pH, all three analytes can be easily oxidized at the surface of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE. Thus, pH 7.0 was identified as a suitable pH level for the simultaneous electrochemical detection of UA, XA, and 5-FU.

The electro-oxidation mechanism of analytes is shown in Scheme 2. Electro-oxidation 5-FU involves the removal of two protons and electrons. Initially, one electron is removed from π electrons of the ring to produce the unstable free radical intermediate, which readily reacts with OH− ions to form the hydroxy-substituted ring. Then, the hydroxy group loss another one electron and proton to get the final oxidized product.

The presence of electron-withdrawing group F in 5-FU inductively attracts the π electrons towards itself. Thus, the removal of the first electron requires higher potential. In this work, N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE oxidizes the 5-FU at 0.96 V, which is the lowest oxidation potential compared to the reported sensors [33,34]. Thus, it evident that the efficient electrocatalytic performance of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE. Similarly, XA's oxidation starts with the loss of π electron of the imine group in the first step. It produces the unstable radical, which further reacts with the OH− ion to produce the intermediate species. Another oxidation takes place by losing one electron and ends up with the final product from this intermediate. UA also loses its π electron during the oxidation, which results in the uric acid imine. Notably, due to the aromatic nature of XA, the oxidation process requires higher potential than UA. Thus, analytes' electrochemical oxidation potential follows the order UA < XA < 5-FU at the surface of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE.

DPV is a more sensitive analytical technique than the CV. Thus, it was performed for the sensitive and selective detection of UA, XA, and 5-FU in their mixture solution. The selective determination of analytes performed at N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE. During the analysis, one analyte's concentration was varied and keeping the other two analytes concentration in a fixed condition. UA's anodic peak current was increased with increasing the UA concentration from 0.5 to 265 μM (Fig. 6a); meanwhile, the concentrations of XA and 5-FU were fixed at 150 and 70 μM, respectively. Similarly, the DPV profiles were recorded for the increasing concentration of XA from 0.5 to 177 μM, while UA's concentration (100 μM) and 5-FU (70 μM) were maintained constantly Fig. 6c. For 5-FU, the concentration was increased from 0.5 to 126 μM, while UA and XA's concentration was maintained at 150 and 100 μM, respectively, and the corresponding DPV results are shown in Fig. 6e. For all three experiments, no obvious interferences were observed. Further, the calibration plots (Fig. 6b, d, f) were drawn between the concentration and the analytes' increasing peak current. From the calibration plot, the limit of detection (LOD) for UA, XA, and 5-FU was found to be 0.106, 0.092, and 0.019 μM with a sensitivity of 1.48, 2.60, and 3.02 μA μM-1, respectively. The linear regression can be expressed as Ipa = 0.2295× + 103.476 (R2 = 0.9930) for UA, Ipa = 0.4082× + 62.963 (R2 = 0.9947), and Ipa = 0.4685× + 116.95 (R2 = 0.9956). Compared with the present work, previous sensing protocols' analytical parameters are tabulated in Table 1–2
. The proposed method exhibits large peak potential separations, a wide linear range with higher sensitivity than the reported sensors.

The simultaneous determination of UA, XA, and 5-FU using the developed sensor was examined by the DPV technique. The concentration of UA (45–125 μM), XA (55–200 μM), and 5-FU (20–90 μM) was simultaneously increased. The obtained results are shown in Fig. 7a; from that, it can be seen that upon increasing the concentration (c (μM)), the anodic peak current (Ipa) also increases. The linear relationship between Ipa and c (μM) of UA, XA, and 5-FU are; Ipa = 0.2138 ± 0.009 [UA] + 100.950 (R2 = 0.9905), Ipa = 0.1589 ± 0.006 [XA] + 93.78 (R2 = 0.9916), and Ipa = 0.1070 ± 0.004 [5-FU] + 117.106 (R2 = 0.9927) respectively (Fig. 7b). The LOD was calculated to be 3.12, 3.71, and 2.51 μM, respectively. As a result, it is evident that the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE can be an efficient prob. for the simultaneous determination of UA, XA, and 5-FU in their ternary mixture solution without any cross-interference.

The interference study (Fig. 8a) of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE towards 100 μM of UA, XA, and 5-FU was verified by adding potentially co-interfering species in 0.1 M, PBS (pH 7.0) at 50 mV s−1. The 10-fold excess concentration of potential interferents such as dopamine (DA), Carbofuran (CF), ascorbic acid (AA), ibuprofen (IBU), catechol (CC), naproxen (NPX), and theophylline (THE) did not make any inference for the simultaneous determination of UA, XA, and 5-FU. Moreover, the interferents make less than 5% of relative error in anodic peak current responses (Fig. 8b-d). Besides, a negligible amount of shift in anodic peak potential was observed, suggesting the excellent anti-interference ability and efficient simultaneous determination capability of the N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE. The sensor's reproducibility was examined by parallel DPV measurements using the five independent modified electrodes (Fig. 9a). Their relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 2.1, 1.8, and 1.4% for UA, XA, and 5-FU, respectively. The operational stability was performed with ten successive measurements (Fig. 9b), and the RSD value was calculated to be 1.8, 1.5, and 1.2% for the three analytes. Likewise, the sensor's storage stability was measured up to one week for the simultaneous determinations, and the observed results illustrate that only 2.7, 2.1, and 1.6% of current responses were decreased from the initial value for UA, XA, and 5-FU, respectively (Fig. 9c). The above results confirm that N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE can be an excellent sensor for simultaneous determination in-terms of efficient performance in selectivity, stability, and reproducibility.

The developed sensor's practicability was tested in human urine (both spiked and un-spiked samples) with similar experimental conditions as of section 3.7. The raw un-spiked urine sample was directly subjected to a sensing experiment without any pre-treatment except the dilution step with PBS (pH 7.0), which gave UA and XA signals, as shown in Fig. 10 , and its concentration was found to be 220 and 65 μM, respectively. Similarly, the urine sample was spiked with UA, XA, and 5-FU known concentrations. The experiment was then performed with the standard addition method, and the obtained analytical results were tabulated in Table 3 . The sensor N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT/GCE provides good recovery performances in the spiked urine sample, demonstrates its suitability and efficiency for real-time applications.

In summary, we have successfully synthesized N-CQD doped Fe2O3 nanocomposite using a simple hydrothermal method and make them as a ternary nanocomposite of N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT by ultrasonication aided process. The graphitic π conjugated structure of N-CQDs made a π - π interaction with MWCNTs and resulted in a stable ternary nanocomposite. Then the simultaneous electrochemical determination of UA, XA, and 5-FU was performed by N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT ternary nanocomposite modified electrode. Well-defined electrochemical anodic peaks were obtained for UA, XA, and 5-FU with peak potential separation of 0.35 V (UA - XA), 0.38 V (XA - 5-FU), and 0.73 V (UA – 5-FU), which were significant enough for simultaneous determination of three analytes. From the CV results, the possible electrochemical reaction mechanism of each analyte was discussed. And then, the effect of concentration, scan rate, and pH was studied and optimized. The modified electrode exhibits well-separated anodic peaks at the optimized conditions corresponding to the oxidation of UA, XA, and 5-FU with the detection limit of 0.106, 0.092, and 0.019 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. The enhanced peak currents were observed at N-CQD@Fe2O3/MWCNT, which evidently proves that the ternary nanocomposite can act as an efficient electrode modifier to boost the kinetics of the electrochemical process. Moreover, the developed sensor exhibits excellent stability, anti-interference stability, and reproducibility. Finally, the sensor was tested with a fresh urine sample from the volunteer and the spiked urine samples. In both samples, excellent recovery results were obtained. The developed sensor showed higher sensitivity, a very low detection limit, and good recovery results towards 5-FU than UA and XA. Thus, it can be a promising platform for the selective and sensitive detection of 5-FU in biological samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge to UGC – BSR (25-1/2014 – 2015(BSR)/7 –88/2007(BSR), Dt.:15th-Oct-2015) fellowship for financial assistance. The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support given by the RUSA-Phase 2.0 grant (vide Letter No. F. 24-51/2014-U (TNMulti-Gen), Department of Education, Govt. of India.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.molliq.2021.115768.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Satyanarayana, K.Y. Goud, K.K. Reddy, K.V. Gobi, Biopolymer stabilized nanogold particles on carbon nanotube support as sensing platform for electrochemical detection of 5-fluorouracil in-vitro, Electrochim. Acta, 178 (2015) 608 – 616.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: An ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor for simultaneous determination of xanthine, hypoxanthine and uric acid based on Co-doped CeO2 nanoparticles
Mater. Sci. Eng. C.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: N. Lavanya, C. Sekar, R. Murugan, G. Ravi, An ultrasensitive electrochemical sensor for simultaneous determination of xanthine, hypoxanthine and uric acid based on Co-doped CeO2 nanoparticles, Mater. Sci. Eng. C. 65 (2016) 278 – 286.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Simultaneous detection of ATP metabolites in human plasma and urine based on palladium nanoparticle and poly(bromocresol green) composite sensor
Biosens. Bioelectron.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Raj, J.-M. Moon, R.N. Goyal, D.-s. Park, Y.-B. Shim, Simultaneous detection of ATP metabolites in human plasma and urine based on palladium nanoparticle and poly(bromocresol green) composite sensor, Biosens. Bioelectron. 126 (2019) 758 – 766.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thiol functionalized carbon ceramic electrode modified with multi-walled carbon nanotubes and gold nanoparticles for simultaneous determination of purine derivatives
Mater. Sci. Eng. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Ferrag, M. Noroozifar, K. kerman, Thiol functionalized carbon ceramic electrode modified with multi-walled carbon nanotubes and gold nanoparticles for simultaneous determination of purine derivatives, Mater. Sci. Eng. C. 110 (2020) 110568.
